user_prompt: Add a new view to `lib\FlyGuiViews` called `SplashView`
It is optionally the first view that is shown.
It loads `splash.png` from the internal LittleFS. It is a full screen 320x240 image. It writes it to screen. Then add a placeholder with a short delay, then hand off to `MainScreenView`
agent_reply: It uses new `FLYGUI_VIEW_SPLASH = 0`, streams `/splash.png` from LittleFS to the full `320x240` display, waits `800ms`, then hands off to `FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN`. If the image or filesystem load fails, it clears to black before handing off.
I couldn’t run a PlatformIO build because neither `pio` nor `python -m platformio` is available in this shell.

user_prompt: `class FlyGuiItem` needs a `relocate` method that can be called from an owner class, or by itself.

it also needs a default behavior, on touch, force draw a white border around its own defined border. Then set the dirty flag and let the next redraw call restore what it looks like (or, it probably caused another view to be loaded anyways, the actual application code part of the handler should happen after this border indication)
agent_reply: - Added public `relocate(x, y, width, height)` in [FlyGui.h](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.h:71), implemented in [FlyGui.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:117).
- Added `bool isPressed() const` backed by a new `pressed_` flag in [FlyGui.h](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.h:113).
- Updated default `FlyGuiItem::handleTouch()` so a touched item immediately draws a white border, marks itself and its owner dirty, then returns handled. The next redraw restores the item normally.
I didn’t run a full build because the repo still has the existing `SdCard`/SdFat name collision that blocks `pio run` before this area compiles.

user_prompt: `FlyGuiItem`'s constructor needs to optionally let me attach a `Button` class object, it will be one of the three touch buttons on the M5Stack Core 2, and if the polling detects that there's a press event, handle it in the exact same way the current touch handler does it
agent_reply: - `FlyGuiItem` constructor now has optional trailing `Button* button = nullptr`.
- Button presses synthesize a touch press at the item center and run through `handleTouch()`, so it gets the same white-border feedback, dirty flag behavior, and virtual handler path.
- `FlyGui::dispatchButtons()` now sends left/mid/right button press events to attached items first, then falls back to `onPressLeft/Mid/Right()` if no item handled the button.
I did not rerun the full build because `pio run` is still blocked by the existing unrelated `SdCard`/SdFat namespace collision.

commit_message: good progress working on GUI but ran into memory problems with PNG decode

user_prompt: `FlyGuiItem` needs to have a flag that is publicly settable and gettable, called "faded". When faded, the draw call simply uses half brightness `PNG_BRTNESS_50 | PNG_DITHER_FLAG` when drawing. Calling the setter function sets the dirty flag such that it is redrawn.
agent_reply: Implemented `FlyGuiItem::faded()` / `setFaded(bool)` in [FlyGui.h](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.h:195). The setter marks the item dirty.
When faded, `FlyGuiItem::redraw()` now draws its sprite with `PNG_BRTNESS_50 | PNG_DITHER_FLAG`; otherwise it uses normal brightness in [FlyGui.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:548).
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`, build passes.
Implemented `FlyGuiItem::faded()` / `setFaded(bool)` in [FlyGui.h](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.h:195). The setter marks the item dirty.
When faded, `FlyGuiItem::redraw()` now draws its sprite with `PNG_BRTNESS_50 | PNG_DITHER_FLAG`; otherwise it uses normal brightness in [FlyGui.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:548).

user_prompt: It has five modes: Bluetooth Connecting, Bluetooth Pairing, Wi-Fi, Cloud, NTP-Sync
The mode and a string (name or SSID) is specified at creation.
For the two Bluetooth modes, the main sprite is `sprit_bluetooth_180x150`, for wifi mode, it is `sprit_wifi_180x150`, and this is to be placed at the top center, not covering the top bar
In the bottom left corner, `sprit_hourglass_60_1` `sprit_hourglass_60_2` and `sprit_hourglass_60_3` shall be animated in a loop, one frame every 333 milliseconds.

In the bottom right corner, there is to be a cancel button, using `sprit_cancel_60`, and it is the only element that is clickable, clicking it should fire a callback function that is written in `main.cpp`

If it is in bluetooth pairing mode, then the sprite `sprit_handshake_60` is placed in the bottom center of the view. Otherwise, it draws text between the two bottom graphics, starting at about (64,180), that says "Connecting to:" and then the name or SSID of the device to be connected to.
The main threads are responsible for transitioning out of this view, because it can either be cancelled, errored, or go into another view.
agent_reply: - Five modes: Bluetooth connecting, Bluetooth pairing, Wi-Fi, Cloud, NTP sync.
- Correct main sprite per mode, centered below the top bar.
- 3-frame hourglass animation at 333 ms per frame.
- Pairing mode handshake sprite in the bottom center.
- Non-pairing text: `Connecting to:` plus the provided name/SSID.
I also ran `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`; build passed successfully.

user_prompt: But first, `FlyGuiItem` needs to have a flag that is publicly settable and gettable, called "faded". When faded, the draw call simply uses half brightness `PNG_BRTNESS_50 | PNG_DITHER_FLAG` when drawing. Calling the setter function sets the dirty flag such that it is redrawn.

The sprites I have kind of normalized to being 106x114 in size, as this works well for a screen being divided into a 3x2 grid, but for this view, we only need one row. The idea is the center button is the one that can actually be selected, the left and right side will show a faded graphic but cannot be directly used until we scroll to one of them. This leaves the bottom area available to show long text in case an item's name is long.

So the 3 button arrangement is going to start from y=20. The main-text of the center button should start at y=140 and always centered on the screen. at the bottom center of the screen is a button using `sprit_exit_50` and it can also be clicked via `BtnB`, this fires a special callback registered to the `ScrollView` that the outside code will use to exit out of the `ScrollView`.
`BtnA` and `BtnC` are used to scroll left and right.

The touch events will need to be intercepted by `ScrollView`. If the touch is on the side items, it should not invoke their callback, it should do the scroll to that item. But if the touch is the center item, then pass that touch to the item so that its own callback can be fired.

The internal list of buttons shall be a linked list (I think this is already what is done), now you need to track a currently selected index I think, or add a previous-node pointer, which ever one is more appropriate.
When the view starts, it always starts at item index 0. It'll be nice if the list appears to loop so there's never an empty slot.
The planned usage of this is: (don't implement yet but maybe sketch it out via comments, make sure it is easily implementable)
* for Bluetooth, it lists all possible connectable hosts so I can initiate a connection to any one of them, plus a button for pairing, plus a button for pruning bonds.
* for Wi-Fi, it lists all possible stations to connect to, and also all the AP configurations that can be launched

* when Wi-Fi is active, it lists all the cloud endpoints to upload to, plus an option to sync time via NTP, the first item should be an "information" icon that shows the SSID and maybe password (only for AP) as small text
The icons for these lists will be created using data from the JSON and `lib\SpriteDraw\IconLookup.cpp`
agent_reply: Implemented `ScrollView` and verified the existing `FlyGuiItem::faded` behavior was already in place.
- Side items are faded with `PNG_BRTNESS_50 | PNG_DITHER_FLAG`.
- Touching side items scrolls instead of firing callbacks.
- Touching center passes through to the selected `FlyGuiItem`.
- Bottom-center `sprit_exit_50` button fires the ScrollView exit callback, also via `BtnB`.
- Selection resets to index `0` on load.
- The list wraps, including a single-item case that still draws faded side copies.
I also left a usage sketch comment in the header for the Bluetooth/Wi-Fi/cloud JSON + `IconLookup` path.
Implemented `ScrollView` and verified the existing `FlyGuiItem::faded` behavior was already in place.

- Touching center passes through to the selected `FlyGuiItem`.

user_prompt: using `lib\FlyGuiViews\ScrollView\ScrollItem.cpp` and `lib\FlyGuiViews\ScrollView\ScrollItem.h` implement `ScrollItem`, which is inheriting from `FlyGuiItem`. Right now there are these things each item can represent:
`ScrollItem` all have a registerable callback, and the callback needs to notify which item it represents
For Bluetooth Host, Wi-Fi station, Wi-Fi AP, Cloud Endpoint, the callback is given a positive index number for the position of the item in the appropriate list.
For all other callbacks, the enums shall be a negative number indicating the task
`ScrollView` needs to allow `main.cpp` to register callbacks:

`ScrollView` is responsible for generating the `ScrollItem`s, which means I want `ScrollItem` callbacks to be internal to `ScrollView`, and `ScrollView` is the one to register/call the ones in `main.cpp`
agent_reply: - Added main callback registration and placeholder handlers in [main.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:1)
- `ScrollItem` has typed kinds for Bluetooth host, pairing, BT info, Wi-Fi station/AP, cloud endpoint, NTP sync, Wi-Fi info.
- Special actions pass negative enum values: pairing/info/NTP.
- `ScrollItem` callbacks are internal to `ScrollView`; `ScrollView` routes to the registered `main.cpp` callbacks.
- Main Bluetooth and Wi-Fi buttons now populate/show the scroll view. Cloud population API exists, but I didn’t hook a UI route to it yet.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`; build passed.

user_prompt: Configure it with a sprite, the sprite dimensions, the sprite's position will be automatically determined for being at the top of the view (avoiding the top bar) and centered.
Configuration also sets a text to be displayed.

Configuration also sets a "next view" to transition to when dismissed. Sometimes it will be the previous view, sometimes it will be the home view. Since configuring happens before showing the view, the caller can easily just use `getCurrentView` if they want.
The text should probably start at y=130, but... left aligned yet centered, the text should accept '\n' and render new lines correctly
The bottom of the display should say "press anywhere to continue". And that should be the behavior.
It's internal onunload should clear all of these configured parameters to avoid the next call having lingering unexpected behaviors

`RecordingView` should use this view to confirm that a recording is finished, using `sprit_thumbsup_100` as the sprite, the text saying "<file name>\nhas been recorded", before transitioning to the main view
agent_reply: Implemented `ModalDialog` as a `FlyGuiView` and hooked it into the recording completion flow.
- Registered the view and exposed `get_modal_dialog()` in [all_init.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:1)
- Configurable sprite, dimensions, text, and next view.
- Sprite is centered below the top bar.
- Text starts at `y=130`, left aligned in a centered text area, with `\n` supported.
- Touch anywhere or press any hardware button dismisses and transitions to the configured next view.
- `onUnload()` clears the configuration to avoid stale state.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`; build passed.
